title: Webex Meeting Analytics
spl:
index=webex sourcetype="webex:meetings"
| stats count as meetings, avg(participant_count) as avg_participants, avg(duration_min) as avg_duration by organizerEmail
| sort -meetings
description: Meeting analytics support collaboration optimization, license management, and quality improvement initiatives.
value: Meeting analytics support collaboration optimization, license management, and quality improvement initiatives.
implementation: Poll Webex API for meeting data. Track meeting counts, participants, duration, and quality. Report on adoption metrics per department. Identify power users and underutilized licenses.
visualization: Bar chart (meetings by department), Line chart (meeting volume trend), Table (usage summary), Pie chart (meeting types).
detailedImplementation:
Prerequisites
• Install and configure the required add-on or app: `ta_cisco_webex_add_on_for_splunk` (GitHub).
• Ensure the following data sources are available: Webex meeting/participant data via API.
• For app installation, inputs.conf, and Splunk directory layout, see the Implementation guide: docs/implementation-guide.md

Step 1 — Configure data collection
Poll Webex API for meeting data. Track meeting counts, participants, duration, and quality. Report on adoption metrics per department. Identify power users and underutilized licenses.

Step 2 — Create the search and alert
Run the following SPL in Search (then save as report or alert; adjust time range and threshold as needed):

```spl
index=webex sourcetype="webex:meetings"
| stats count as meetings, avg(participant_count) as avg_participants, avg(duration_min) as avg_duration by organizerEmail
| sort -meetings
```

Understanding this SPL

**Webex Meeting Analytics** — Meeting analytics support collaboration optimization, license management, and quality improvement initiatives.

Documented **Data sources**: Webex meeting/participant data via API. **App/TA** (typical add-on context): `ta_cisco_webex_add_on_for_splunk` (GitHub). The SPL below should target the same indexes and sourcetypes you configured for that feed—rename `index=` / `sourcetype=` if your deployment differs.

The first pipeline stage scopes events using **index**: webex; **sourcetype**: webex:meetings. If that sourcetype is not mentioned in Data sources, double-check parsing or update the documentation to match the feed you actually ingest.

**Pipeline walkthrough**

• Scopes the data: index=webex, sourcetype="webex:meetings". Cross-check against **Data sources** above so indexes and sourcetypes match your ingestion.
• `stats` rolls up events into metrics; results are split **by organizerEmail** so each row reflects one combination of those dimensions.
• Orders rows with `sort` — combine with `head`/`tail` for top-N patterns.


Step 3 — Validate
Compare with Cisco Webex Control Hub analytics, audit, and service status for the same time range.

Step 4 — Operationalize
Add the search to a dashboard or set up alert actions (email, webhook, PagerDuty, etc.) as required. Document the use case in your runbook and assign an owner. Consider visualizations: Bar chart (meetings by department), Line chart (meeting volume trend), Table (usage summary), Pie chart (meeting types).
grandmaExplanation: We use this to stay ahead of email and collaboration problems, so the team is not the last to know when something drifts or breaks.
hardware: Cisco Webex Calling, Webex Meetings, Webex Room Kit, Webex Board, Webex Desk
knownFalsePositives: Executive briefings, webinars, and all-hands that spike meeting analytics without a fault.
dataModelAcceleration: Not applicable; primary SPL uses raw or vendor-normalized event fields.
